summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft's decision to cut its Windows Store gaming commission from 30% to 12% while maintaining the 30% cut on Xbox exposes the hypocrisy of companies like Epic and Microsoft who criticize Apple's 70/30 split. He rejects the argument that console platforms are inherently different from mobile platforms, contending that Microsoft keeps 30% on Xbox simply because it's a closed system with no competing stores, not because of any principled business model distinction. He draws direct price comparisons between consoles and Apple devices to undermine the hardware-subsidy justification. The core argument is that you can't credibly claim 70/30 is unfair on iOS while accepting it on gaming consoles.
key_points: Microsoft cut its Windows Store gaming commission to 12% because it faces competition from Steam, not because of a different business model philosophy
Xbox maintains 30% because it's a closed platform with no alternative stores — the same structural position as iOS
Price comparisons between consoles ($250-500) and Apple devices ($200-400) undermine the argument that console hardware subsidies justify a different commission standard
Microsoft testified they've never profited from Xbox hardware sales, but Gruber argues this doesn't create any legal or ethical obligation for profitable hardware makers to lower software commissions
The razor-and-blades business model doesn't obligate companies that profit on hardware to charge less for software
money_quotes: Competition from Steam (which still takes 30 percent from game sales) drove Microsoft to lower its commission in the Windows Store.
There is no Steam for Xbox, nor any other alternatives.
Microsoft would be a lot more likely to lower its commission on Xbox games in its own Xbox store if they also allowed alternative app stores or subscription game-streaming services on Xbox.

Just because most companies make no money selling razors but reap profits by selling blades at high margins doesn't mean a company that does sell its razors for a profit is legally or even ethically obligated to sell its blades for lower margins.
You can argue that the 70/30 split is too high, but it holds no water to argue that it's too high only for iOS and Android, but not for Xbox, PlayStation, and Switch.
How exactly is the $300-ish Nintendo Switch a different thing altogether than a $300-ish iPad or iPod Touch?
key_insight: The distinction between console and mobile platform commission rates is driven not by principled business model differences but by whether a platform faces competition from alternative stores within its own ecosystem.
